ground_truth.title: Something Big Happened in 1998
ground_truth.author: Chad Whitacre
ground_truth.main_content: 37signals recently launched a source-available software product that they choose to call open source, in open defiance of the Open Source Initiative's Open Source Definition. The author opened a PR inviting 37signals to join Fair Source, the initiative they launched a year ago. Detractors of OSI sometimes attempt to undermine them by contesting the history of the term 'open source,' claiming it was already in wide circulation by the time OSI sought to institutionalize a specific definition. This is false. While there were a handful of outlier references to 'open source' prior to OSI, 'wide circulation' there was not. Charts from Google Books show a clear explosion in usage of 'open source' coincident with the founding of OSI in February 1998. The pattern is similar in magazines: effectively nobody was talking about Open Source until OSI introduced it with the Open Source Definition. Analysis of ~53 million Usenet posts to comp.* newsgroups from 1986 to 2013 found a clear explosion at OSI's founding: 0.004% of posts mentioned the term pre-OSI, compared with 0.686% post-OSI, a 19,282% increase. The Hecker Memo illustrates this watershed: the post-OSI version has 305 usages of 'open source' bound to the OSD, while the pre-OSI original has exactly one. Something big happened in 1998. It is reasonable to accept that OSI founded the Open Source movement with the Open Source Definition.
with: Something big happened in 1998. Whatever your opinion of events since, it's reasonable to accept that the Open Source Initiative founded the Open Source movement with the Open Source Definition.
Charts from Google Books show a clear explosion in usage of 'open source' coincident with the founding of the Open Source Initiative.
Analysis of ~53 million posts to the comp.* newsgroups found a clear explosion in usage of the term 'open source' at the founding of OSI.

In the post-OSI version of Frank's memo, I count 305 usages of the term 'open source,' where of course its meaning is bound to the then-freshly minted Open Source Definition. In the pre-OSI original? Exactly one.
This debate must be won by reason and suasion, not harassment and naked claims to authority.
